quote.text: War is merely the continuation of policy by other means.
quote.author: Carl von Clausewitz, On War (1832)

macro.title: What It Means — VWRA, Global Bonds, Gold & BTC

reads.label: VWRA
reads.text: the index is pricing calibrated conflict, not peace — it rises with every strike that spares Kharg and will fall the day one doesn't; the deductible is smaller, not gone.

reads.label: Global bonds
reads.text: genuinely better week — hike odds eased and Tokyo's long end rallied; the ballast is breathing again, pending Tuesday's CPI.

reads.label: Gold
reads.text: the bounce confirms the regime rather than escaping it — real yields still set the ceiling; $4,123 is a floor test passed, not a breakout.

reads.label: BTC
reads.text: below $64k the story is liquidity, and this week liquidity was the KOSPI's, not bitcoin's.

macro.note: Education, not advice; forecasts are forecasts.

stories.headline: The reply was addressed to be intercepted
stories.dek: Hours after burying its leader, Iran fired ten missiles and a wave of drones at four US-allied states — nearly all shot down, which may have been the intention. Ninety American targets later, the one that matters stands untouched. The talks never stopped.

stories.headline: Korea's crown jewel rings the bell in New York
stories.dek: SK hynix priced its American depositary shares at $149 on Thursday — a premium to Seoul, seven times oversubscribed, $26.5 billion raised: the largest US listing by a foreign company in history. Two days after the market torched the memory sector, it paid up to own it.

stories.kicker: The Macro Desk · Four Instruments, One Reading
stories.headline: The market read the target list
stories.dek: On the second day of American strikes, equities rose, oil fell and gold recovered — a combination that looks perverse until you notice what the market noticed: the one target that wasn't on the list. Restraint, it turns out, is a price signal.

stories.headline: Half a trillion, kept a quarter liquid
stories.dek: Temasek's portfolio hit a record S$518 billion the same week the Straits Times Index closed above 5,400 for the first time and DBS crossed seventy dollars. The state investor's most telling number, though, isn't the record — it's how much it keeps ready for the day the record stops.

stories.headline: The gallery doors open at three thousand a foot
stories.dek: Dunearn House opens its showflat this morning with the full price list now public — and the bigger units carry a number Bukit Timah has never asked for. Across town, a seventy-two-unit freehold tries the en-bloc market for the fourth time.

stories.headline: The $99 tracker that wants to disappear
stories.dek: Google's Fitbit Air shipped in late May; the review verdicts have now landed, and they're warmer than the spec sheet suggested. A five-gram screenless pebble is making a quiet argument the reader — who wears a ring on one hand and a strap on the other — already believes: the best tracker is the one you forget.

stories.headline: The first Matter camera works in exactly one app
stories.dek: Aqara's Camera Hub G350 is drawing the best reviews of any camera the company has made — and it carries a milestone certification whose promise, today, is honoured by a single platform. The gap between "certified" and "works in your dashboard" is this desk's oldest lesson, freshly illustrated.

stories.headline: The cocktail hour, priced — and a two-minute sell-out
stories.dek: The Presage refresh we held for want of a price now has one, and it lands where Seiko is most dangerous: under five hundred euros with nothing missing. Meanwhile a preppy collaboration sold its entire run before the coffee cooled, and a London microbrand is chiselling cases by hand.

stories.headline: The remake sails into a reef
stories.dek: Disney's live-action Moana opens tomorrow carrying the worst reviews any of its theatrical remakes has ever taken to sea — in the same week Christopher Nolan's Odyssey premiered to something close to rapture. Two ways to spend a fortune on a familiar story; only one of them held anything back.

stories.kicker: The Family Desk · The Peer Scaffold
stories.headline: Friendship is the intervention
stories.dek: A new national report on girlhood opens with the bleak numbers everyone expects — half of tween girls already want to change how they look — and then makes an unfashionable argument: the strongest lever isn't a rule, an app or a programme. It's whether a friend is standing next to her.

stories.headline: The first fossil collector wore a toga
stories.dek: In a second-century Roman rubbish pit in Colchester, archaeologists found a vertebra that had already been dead for 180 million years when its owner picked it up. Someone in Roman Britain collected a sea monster — and became, as far as the record knows, the first of us to do it.

stories.headline: It's the logistics, not the pride
stories.dek: Wednesday's study said the old story about higher-earning wives didn't hold. Now a far bigger dataset — half a million couples across twenty-nine countries — says what actually does: not wounded masculinity, but the unabsorbed shock of who covers the childcare when the earner can't.

stories.headline: The circuit that turns deep sleep into repair
stories.dek: Berkeley neuroscientists have mapped the wiring that connects slow-wave sleep to growth-hormone release — with a feedback loop nobody expected. For anyone who wears a recovery ring to bed, it's the mechanism behind the number you check every morning.

stories.headline: What's worth booking
stories.dek: A metal band returns after eleven years, K-pop stakes out 2027's calendar, Bruno Mars opens a Tokyo Dome lottery — and the National Day fireworks start tomorrow. Plus the on-sale this city still cannot buy.

stories.headline: Fight, fight; talk, talk
stories.dek: This week missiles were fired to be intercepted, ninety targets were struck so that one could be conspicuously spared, and the negotiators never hung up the phone. This looks like madness and is in fact a grammar — one with a long history, exacting rules, and a single catastrophic failure mode: being read literally.
